text: Hi, it's Dr. Harris from California Hernia Specialists. A lot of patients come in our office and ask about robotic surgery for inguinal hernias. So I thought I'd put together a couple of our videos that I took recently during a laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair. What I tell patients in the office is robotic surgery is an amazing advancement in medicine and in surgery, but really only applies to complicated pelvic surgery and other complicated surgeries that require specific attention to all of the nerves, arteries, veins of the pelvis or of the liver or of the pancreas. And when we're doing inguinal hernia repairs, although we're very careful to avoid the nerves and the arteries, we're not dissecting those nerves out and preserving them because they're not in and around the hernia. So what I wanted to demonstrate today, I wanted to show you basically an unedited version of our two most recent laparoscopic inguinal hernia repairs. And I would certainly challenge any of the robotic surgeons who do robotic inguinal hernia repairs to whether or not their technique is better than this. There's just no way that a robotic surgeon can bring in the robot, what we call dock the robot, which is connecting it to all the different arms, then actually doing the surgery, and then disconnecting the robot and moving it out of the area, and then closing everything up in the same amount of time that we would be able to do a laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair. I hope you enjoyed this video. We'll put together a second one as well. And certainly if you have any questions or concerns, feel free to give us a call in our office. We're always happy to help. Thanks.

The surgeon prepares for a laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair by marking the incision site on the left side of the abdomen. After administering numbing medication at the belly button, a small incision is made. The rectus sheath is opened, and the rectus muscle is retracted to the side. A trocar and balloon are inserted next to the muscle into the pelvic area where the hernia is located.

The surgeon makes a small incision at the belly button, inserting a retractor and a collapsed balloon through it. The balloon is inflated to push the intestines and peritoneum aside, revealing the muscle inside the hernia. The camera is inserted, and the balloon is further inflated on the left side. After removing the balloon, the trocar is connected to gas insufflation to expand the area for better visibility. The rectus muscle and connective tissue are visible. Local anesthetic is administered, and two small punctures are made for inserting working trocars between the rectus muscles. Instruments are prepared for the surgery.

We're starting to look for the hernia. If a surgeon was doing the robotic surgery, at this actually bring the robot over the patient, sterilize the robot, and they wrap it in plastic. They bring it over the patient. They connect the robotic arms to special instruments. And then the surgeon, once those are all connected, he actually, we call scrubs out of the case. He takes off his his gown, his gloves, and he walks over to a remote machine in the corner of the room and then he looks through that robot control center and he's looking through the connected to the camera, which is inside of the patient. So even if that goes quickly, that's probably about a five to ten minute process before he even starts the surgery. And as you'll be able to see, and if you've started your stopwatch, you'll see that this surgery is going to be over by the time he starts. Again, what we're doing here is we're not even around any of the nerves. We're not around any of the arteries. You can see that little white

The surgeon uses a clamp to push away the vas deferens, a tube connecting the testis to the prostate. The hernia sac, identified as the peritoneum protruding through the muscle, is held with a grasper in the left hand. The surgeon carefully teases the hernia sac out of the groin and back into its proper position at the bottom of the screen, avoiding damage to nearby arteries and veins.

The surgeon carefully dissects the hernia sac from the cord structures, including the vas deferens and microscopic arteries and veins leading to the testicle. The sac is peeled away from the internal ring, visible at the top of the screen, and the peritoneum is stripped off, revealing the pubic bone at the top right of the screen. The bladder is pushed aside to ensure the peritoneum is completely removed from the area.

The surgeon prepares and inserts a rolled mesh into the surgical site, positioning it over the internal ring and the floor of the inguinal canal. Using two graspers, the mesh is unrolled and secured with dissolvable tacks near the pubic bone and the patient's side to cover the inguinal region, aiming to prevent future hernias.

The surgeon places tacks along the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament to secure the mesh over the internal ring and the floor of the inguinal canal, preventing future direct hernias. The surgeon evacuates gas from the abdominal cavity using a trocar to minimize postoperative discomfort. The trocars are removed, and the muscle is closed, marking the end of the laparoscopic hernia repair procedure.
